metadata.validation: SRS-aware with placeholder blocking and deduplication

phase1_test_cases.test_title: SQL Injection in Mobile App Download
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate SQL injection vulnerability in mobile app download feature.
preconditions: Mobile app download page is accessible
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to mobile app download page at /download
Step 2: Enter username 'admin' in email field and payload '' OR 1=1 in password field
Step 3: Send the malicious request
Step 4: Verify that the response contains a database syntax error indicating SQL injection
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application returns database syntax error indicating the parameter is vulnerable to SQL injection.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Weak Authentication in Mobile App Download
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate weak authentication vulnerability in mobile app download feature.
preconditions: Mobile app download page is accessible
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to mobile app download page at /download
Step 2: Enter username 'admin' in email field and password 'password'
Step 3: Send the request
Step 4: Verify that the response indicates successful authentication
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application logs in successfully without requiring valid credentials, indicating weak authentication.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Sensitive Data Exposure in Mobile App Download
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate sensitive data exposure vulnerability in mobile app download feature.
preconditions: Mobile app download page is accessible
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to mobile app download page at /download
Step 2: Send the request
Step 3: Capture the HTTP response using Burp Suite
Step 4: Verify that sensitive data such as API keys or passwords are exposed
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application exposes sensitive data such as API keys or passwords, indicating lack of encryption.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Privilege Escalation in Mobile App Download
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate privilege escalation vulnerability in mobile app download feature.
preconditions: Mobile app download page is accessible
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to mobile app download page at /download
Step 2: Enter username 'admin' in email field and password 'password'
Step 3: Send the request
Step 4: Verify that the response indicates access to restricted features
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application grants unauthorized access to restricted features, indicating privilege escalation vulnerability.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: SQL Injection: Download mobile application parameter
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate SQL injection vulnerability in download mobile application parameter
preconditions: Application is accessible via web interface
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to /download-mobile-app
Step 2: Enter payload ' OR 1=1 --' as the download link
Step 3: Send the malicious request
Step 4: Verify that the response contains a list of available mobile apps
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application returns a list of available mobile apps, indicating successful SQL injection exploitation

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Authentication Bypass: Download mobile application login
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate authentication bypass vulnerability in download mobile application login
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to /login
Step 2: Enter username 'admin' and password '' OR '1'='1'
Step 3: Send the malicious request
Step 4: Verify that the response redirects to the admin dashboard
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application redirects to the admin dashboard, indicating successful authentication bypass exploitation

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Sensitive Data Exposure: Download mobile application parameters
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate sensitive data exposure vulnerability in download mobile application parameters
preconditions: Application has a parameter for storing sensitive information
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to /download-mobile-app
Step 2: Enter payload 'sensitive_data=secret' as the download link
Step 3: Send the malicious request
Step 4: Verify that the response contains the sensitive data
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application returns the sensitive data, indicating successful exposure of sensitive information

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Privilege Escalation: Download mobile application permissions
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate privilege escalation vulnerability in download mobile application permissions
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to /download-mobile-app
Step 2: Enter payload 'permission_level=high' as the download link
Step 3: Send the malicious request
Step 4: Verify that the response indicates elevated permissions
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application grants elevated permissions, indicating successful privilege escalation exploitation

phase1_test_cases.test_title: SQL Injection in Search Parameter
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate SQL injection vulnerability in search parameter
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to search page at /search
Step 2: Enter payload ' OR 1=1' in the search query field
Step 3: Send the malicious request and capture HTTP response using Burp Suite
Step 4: Verify that the response contains sensitive data indicating successful exploitation
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application returns a list of all users, indicating SQL injection vulnerability in search parameter

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Session Hijacking in Login Page
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate session hijacking vulnerability in login page
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to login page at /login
Step 2: Capture the HTTP POST request using Burp Suite for a legitimate login attempt
Step 3: Modify the captured request to include an existing session ID and send it
Step 4: Verify that the application logs in with the hijacked session, indicating successful exploitation
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application logs in with the hijacked session ID, indicating session hijacking vulnerability in login page

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Unencrypted Sensitive Data Exposure in User Profile
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate unencrypted sensitive data exposure in user profile page
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to user profile page at /profile
Step 2: Capture the HTTP request using Burp Suite for a legitimate profile view
Step 3: Verify that sensitive data such as credit card numbers or passwords are not encrypted in the response
Step 4: Verify that the application does not prompt for re-authentication, indicating successful exploitation
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application returns unencrypted sensitive data such as credit card numbers or passwords in user profile page

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Privilege Escalation in User Management Page
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate privilege escalation vulnerability in user management page
preconditions: User is logged in with limited privileges
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to user management page at /users
Step 2: Capture the HTTP request using Burp Suite for a legitimate user list view
Step 3: Modify the captured request to include an administrator's credentials and send it
Step 4: Verify that the application grants elevated privileges, indicating successful exploitation
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application grants elevated privileges to a user with limited privileges, indicating privilege escalation vulnerability in user management page

phase1_test_cases.test_title: SQL Injection in Restaurant Menu Search
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate SQL injection vulnerability in restaurant menu search functionality.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the restaurant menu page at /menu
Step 2: Enter payload ' OR 1=1 --' in the search field for dishes
Step 3: Send the malicious request and capture the HTTP response using Burp Suite
Step 4: Verify that the response contains a list of all dishes, indicating successful exploitation
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application returns a list of all menu items, indicating SQL injection vulnerability.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Session Hijacking in Restaurant Login
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate session hijacking vulnerability in restaurant login functionality.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the login page at /login
Step 2: Capture the HTTP POST request using Burp Suite for a legitimate login attempt
Step 3: Modify the captured request to include an existing session ID and send it
Step 4: Verify that the application logs in the attacker as the legitimate user, indicating successful exploitation
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application logs in the attacker as the legitimate user, indicating session hijacking vulnerability.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Exposure of Restaurant User Data
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate exposure of sensitive restaurant user data.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the user profile page at /user/profile
Step 2: Capture the HTTP response using Burp Suite for a legitimate user's profile
Step 3: Verify that the response contains sensitive user data, such as email address or phone number
Step 4: Verify that the application does not encrypt or hash the sensitive data
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application exposes sensitive restaurant user data in plain text, indicating lack of encryption.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Privilege Escalation in Restaurant Menu Management
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate privilege escalation vulnerability in restaurant menu management functionality.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the menu management page at /menu/manage
Step 2: Capture the HTTP response using Burp Suite for a legitimate user's menu management request
Step 3: Modify the captured request to include elevated privileges and send it
Step 4: Verify that the application grants the attacker elevated privileges, indicating successful exploitation
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application grants the attacker elevated privileges in menu management, indicating privilege escalation vulnerability.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: SQL Injection in Information Link Selection
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate SQL injection vulnerability in selecting information link.
preconditions: Information link selection page is accessible
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the information link selection page at /info-links
Step 2: Enter payload ' OR 1=1 --' into the search field
Step 3: Send the malicious request and capture the HTTP response using Burp Suite
Step 4: Verify that the response contains sensitive user data indicating SQL injection
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application returns database syntax error indicating the parameter is vulnerable to SQL injection.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Session Fixation in Information Link Selection
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate session fixation vulnerability in selecting information link.
preconditions: Information link selection page is accessible
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the login page at /login
Step 2: Enter username 'admin' and password 'password123'
Step 3: Log in and capture the HTTP response using Burp Suite
Step 4: Modify the session ID in the captured request to a known value
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application logs user in with the fixed session ID, indicating session fixation.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Unencrypted Sensitive Data Exposure in Information Link Selection
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate unencrypted sensitive data exposure vulnerability in selecting information link.
preconditions: Information link selection page is accessible
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the information link selection page at /info-links
Step 2: Capture the HTTP request using Burp Suite
Step 3: Verify that sensitive user data, such as credit card numbers or passwords, are transmitted in plain text
Step 4: Observe the lack of encryption indicators in the captured request
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application transmits sensitive user data in plain text, indicating unencrypted exposure.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Privilege Escalation in Information Link Selection
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate privilege escalation vulnerability in selecting information link.
preconditions: User is logged in with limited privileges
Information link selection page is accessible
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the information link selection page at /info-links
Step 2: Capture the HTTP request using Burp Suite
Step 3: Modify the request to impersonate a user with elevated privileges
Step 4: Verify that the application grants access to restricted information or functionality
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application grants access to restricted information or functionality, indicating privilege escalation.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Session Hijacking in Price Range Search
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate session hijacking vulnerability in price range search feature.
preconditions: Price range search page is accessible
test_steps: Step 1: Log in to the application as a valid user
Step 2: Capture the HTTP POST request using Burp Suite for the login form
Step 3: Modify the captured request to include an existing session ID, e.g., 'JSESSIONID=1234567890'
Step 4: Submit the modified request and verify that the application logs in as the hijacked user
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application logs in as the hijacked user, indicating successful session hijacking.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Unencrypted Sensitive Data Exposure in Price Range Search
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate exposure of sensitive data, such as credit card numbers or passwords.
preconditions: Price range search page is accessible
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the price range search page at /search/prices
Step 2: Input a valid minimum and maximum price range, e.g., $10.00 - $50.00
Step 3: Inspect the HTTP response using Burp Suite or another tool
Step 4: Verify that sensitive data is exposed in the response
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application returns unencrypted credit card numbers, indicating successful exposure of sensitive data.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Privilege Escalation in Price Range Search
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate privilege escalation vulnerability in price range search feature.
preconditions: User is logged in with limited privileges
Price range search page is accessible
test_steps: Step 1: Log in to the application as a user with limited privileges, e.g., 'user'
Step 2: Navigate to the price range search page at /search/prices
Step 3: Input a valid minimum and maximum price range, e.g., $10.00 - $50.00
Step 4: Verify that the application returns results indicating successful privilege escalation
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application returns menu items with elevated privileges, indicating successful privilege escalation.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Session Fixation in Mobile App's Search Feature
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate session fixation vulnerability in mobile app's search feature.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to mobile app's login page
Step 2: Enter username 'admin' and password 'password123'
Step 3: Log in and note the session ID
Step 4: Log out, then log back in with a new session ID
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application logs user back in with the same session ID, indicating successful session fixation.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Privilege Escalation in Search by Destination

phase1_test_cases.test_title: SQL Injection: Price Search Parameter
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate that the price search parameter is vulnerable to SQL injection attacks.
preconditions: User has logged in and is on the search page
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the search page at /search
Step 2: Enter a valid price range (e.g., 10-20) in the 'Price' field
Step 3: Modify the 'Price' field to inject the payload ' OR 1=1 --'
Step 4: Submit the search form and verify that the response contains multiple results
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: The application returns a list of multiple menu items, indicating successful SQL injection.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Session Fixation: Destination Search Parameter
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate that the destination search parameter is vulnerable to session fixation attacks.
preconditions: User has logged in and is on the search page
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the search page at /search
Step 2: Enter a valid destination (e.g., 'New York') in the 'Destination' field
Step 3: Modify the 'Destination' field to inject the payload 'sessionid=12345'
Step 4: Submit the search form and verify that the response contains the user's session ID
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: The application sets the session ID to '12345' without validating the input, indicating successful session fixation.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Unencrypted Sensitive Data: User Information
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate that the user information is not encrypted and can be accessed by an attacker.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the user profile page at /user/profile
Step 2: Verify that the user's sensitive information (e.g., credit card number) is displayed
Step 3: Capture the HTTP request using Burp Suite
Step 4: Forward the request and observe the HTTP response
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: The application displays the user's sensitive information in plain text, indicating successful exposure of sensitive data.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Privilege Escalation: Menu Item Access
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate that a user can access menu items without proper authorization.
preconditions: User has logged in with a standard account
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the menu page at /menu
Step 2: Verify that the user's standard account cannot access premium menu items
Step 3: Modify the HTTP request to inject the payload 'role=admin'
Step 4: Submit the modified request and verify that the application grants access to premium menu items
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: The application grants access to premium menu items without proper authorization, indicating successful privilege escalation.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Privilege Escalation in Destination Search

phase1_test_cases.test_title: SQL Injection in Search by Restaurant Type
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate SQL injection vulnerability in search by restaurant type feature.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to /search/restaurant-type with parameter type=
Step 2: Enter payload ' OR 1=1 --' as the value for the type parameter
Step 3: Send the malicious request and capture the HTTP response using Burp Suite
Step 4: Verify that the response contains a list of all restaurant types, indicating successful exploitation
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application returns a list of all restaurant types, bypassing intended search functionality.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Session Fixation in Search by Restaurant Type
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate session fixation vulnerability in search by restaurant type feature.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to /search/restaurant-type with parameter type=
Step 2: Set the JSESSIONID cookie to a fixed value using Burp Suite
Step 3: Send a GET request to /search/restaurant-type with the fixed JSESSIONID cookie
Step 4: Verify that the application logs in the user without prompting for credentials
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application logs in the user without prompting for credentials, indicating successful session fixation.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Exposure of Sensitive Data in Search by Restaurant Type
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate exposure of sensitive data in search by restaurant type feature.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to /search/restaurant-type with parameter type=
Step 2: Send a GET request to /api/search-restaurant-types with the type parameter set to a specific value
Step 3: Capture the HTTP response using Burp Suite and inspect the payload
Step 4: Verify that the response contains sensitive data, such as restaurant names or addresses
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application returns a list of Italian restaurants with their names and addresses, indicating exposure of sensitive data.

phase1_test_cases.test_title: Privilege Escalation in Search by Restaurant Type
phase1_test_cases.description: Validate privilege escalation vulnerability in search by restaurant type feature.
preconditions: User is logged in with a standard user role
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to /search/restaurant-type with parameter type=
Step 2: Send a GET request to /api/search-restaurant-types with the type parameter set to a specific value and an administrator's session cookie
Step 3: Capture the HTTP response using Burp Suite and inspect the payload
Step 4: Verify that the response contains data accessible only to administrators, indicating successful privilege escalation
phase1_test_cases.expected_result: Application returns a list of Italian restaurants with their names and addresses, including data accessible only to administrators, indicating successful privilege escalation.

Step 4: Verify that the authenticated session is persisted across subsequent requests.
Step 5: Attempt to access sensitive data or perform unauthorized actions within the authenticated session.

preconditions: Mobile application is accessible via public network
test_steps: Step 1: Access the mobile application's login page and verify that the 'Content-Security-Policy' header is missing or not properly configured.
Step 2: Craft a payload to test the effectiveness of the missing security headers, such as sending an HTTP request with a malicious script tag.
Step 3: Submit the payload and verify that the application does not block the malicious script execution due to the missing 'Content-Security-Policy' header.
Step 4: Verify that the application is vulnerable to cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks.
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The mobile application does not block malicious script execution due to missing 'Content-Security-Policy' header, indicating vulnerability to XSS attacks.

Step 2: Craft a payload to test the verbosity of error messages, such as sending an invalid request parameter.
Step 3: Submit the payload and verify that the application returns a verbose error message revealing system information (e.g., server software version).
Step 4: Verify that the error message contains sensitive information about the underlying technology stack or system configuration.
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The mobile application returns a verbose error message revealing system information, such as server software version or technology stack, indicating vulnerability to information disclosure attacks.

test_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to the Search by Price API with a serialized malicious payload in the 'price' parameter
Step 2: Verify that the API returns a successful response (HTTP 200) and logs an error message indicating deserialization failure
Step 3: Craft a custom Java class that, when deserialized, executes arbitrary code on the server-side
Step 4: Modify the 'price' parameter in the previous request to contain the malicious serialized payload
Step 5: Send the modified request and verify that the API returns an error message indicating successful execution of the malicious code
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The API returns an error message indicating successful execution of the malicious code, and the server-side logs show evidence of arbitrary code execution

phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The API returns an error message indicating successful execution of the malicious code, and the server-side logs show evidence of arbitrary code execution

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Outdated OpenSSL version in mobile app
phase2_test_cases.description: Exploit the outdated OpenSSL version to perform a man-in-the-middle attack and steal sensitive information.
test_steps: Step 1: Identify the OpenSSL version used by the mobile app using the 'openssl version' command
Step 2: Verify that the identified OpenSSL version is outdated and vulnerable to known attacks
Step 3: Create a custom SSL certificate with a self-signed CA and use it to create a man-in-the-middle attack on the mobile app's communication
Step 4: Intercept the mobile app's HTTPS requests and inject malicious data into the communication stream
Step 5: Verify that the injected data is successfully received by the mobile app and can be used for further exploitation
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: Successful injection of malicious data into the communication stream, allowing for further exploitation of sensitive information (minimum 30 characters)

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability in mobile app
phase2_test_cases.description: Exploit the unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability to perform a remote code execution attack and gain control of the mobile app.
test_steps: Step 1: Identify the version of Apache Struts used by the mobile app using the 'struts.version' property
Step 2: Verify that the identified version is vulnerable to known attacks and has not been patched
Step 3: Craft a custom payload exploiting the vulnerability and send it to the mobile app's API endpoint
Step 4: Intercept the response from the mobile app and verify that the payload has been successfully executed
Step 5: Verify that the mobile app is now under attacker control, allowing for further exploitation
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: Successful execution of payload and gain of control over the mobile app (minimum 30 characters)

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Outdated SQLite version in mobile app
phase2_test_cases.description: Exploit the outdated SQLite version to perform a SQL injection attack and gain access to sensitive information.
test_steps: Step 1: Identify the version of SQLite used by the mobile app using the 'sqlite_version' property
Step 2: Verify that the identified version is outdated and vulnerable to known attacks
Step 3: Craft a custom payload exploiting the vulnerability and send it to the mobile app's API endpoint
Step 4: Intercept the response from the mobile app and verify that the payload has been successfully executed
Step 5: Verify that sensitive information has been accessed and can be used for further exploitation
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: Successful access to sensitive information using SQL injection attack (minimum 30 characters)

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Login Log Injection Vulnerability
phase2_test_cases.description: This test attempts to exploit a log injection vulnerability in the login functionality of the mobile application.
test_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the login endpoint with a valid username and password combination, capturing the response
Step 2: Modify the 'username' parameter in the captured response to contain a malicious log injection payload (e.g., '; INSERT INTO logs VALUES (')
Step 3: Resubmit the modified request to the login endpoint, verifying that the application does not detect or prevent the injected payload
Step 4: Inspect the application's logs for evidence of the injected payload, such as an unexpected database query or log entry
Step 5: Verify that the application's logging mechanism is configured to capture and store the injected payload
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The application's logs contain evidence of the injected payload, such as an unexpected database query or log entry (minimum 30 characters)

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Server Error Log Lack of Auditing
phase2_test_cases.description: This test attempts to exploit the lack of auditing for server errors in the mobile application.
test_steps: Step 1: Send a request to an invalid API endpoint (e.g., /api/non-existent-endpoint) to trigger a server error
Step 2: Verify that the application does not log or audit the server error, such as by inspecting the logs for any relevant entries
Step 3: Modify the request to include a malicious payload (e.g., a SQL injection attack) to further test the logging mechanism's effectiveness
Step 4: Resubmit the modified request and verify that the application does not detect or prevent the injected payload
Step 5: Inspect the application's logs for evidence of the server error and any potential security incidents
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The application's logs do not contain evidence of the server error or any relevant auditing information (minimum 30 characters)

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Brute Force Attack Alert Lack
phase2_test_cases.description: This test attempts to exploit the lack of alerting for brute force attacks in the mobile application.
test_steps: Step 1: Send a series of login requests with incorrect credentials (e.g., multiple failed login attempts) to trigger a brute force attack
Step 2: Verify that the application does not detect or alert on the brute force attack, such as by inspecting the logs for any relevant entries
Step 3: Modify the request to include a malicious payload (e.g., a SQL injection attack) to further test the logging mechanism's effectiveness
Step 4: Resubmit the modified request and verify that the application does not detect or prevent the injected payload
Step 5: Inspect the application's logs for evidence of the brute force attack and any potential security incidents
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The application's logs do not contain evidence of the brute force attack or any relevant alerting information (minimum 30 characters)

phase2_test_cases.test_title: SSRF: Internal IP Address Disclosure via Search by Price
phase2_test_cases.description: This test case attempts to exploit the SSRF vulnerability in the Mobile application's search functionality to disclose internal IP addresses.
preconditions: The mobile application is installed and running on a device
test_steps: Step 1: Launch the mobile application and navigate to the Search by Price feature
Step 2: Craft a malicious search query that includes an SSRF payload, for example, `http://10.0.0.1:8080/`
Step 3: Submit the search query and observe the application's response
Step 4: Verify that the application makes a request to the internal IP address (10.0.0.1:8080) instead of the expected external API endpoint
Step 5: Analyze the response from the internal IP address to identify any sensitive information disclosed
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The application makes a request to the internal IP address (10.0.0.1:8080) and discloses sensitive information, such as internal IP addresses or metadata APIs.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: SSRF: AWS IMDS Disclosure via Search by Price
phase2_test_cases.description: This test case attempts to exploit the SSRF vulnerability in the Mobile application's search functionality to disclose metadata APIs, specifically AWS IMDS.
preconditions: The mobile application is installed and running on a device
test_steps: Step 1: Launch the mobile application and navigate to the Search by Price feature
Step 2: Craft a malicious search query that includes an SSRF payload, for example, `http://metadata.ims.amazonaws.com/`
Step 3: Submit the search query and observe the application's response
Step 4: Verify that the application makes a request to the AWS IMDS metadata API instead of the expected external API endpoint
Step 5: Analyze the response from the AWS IMDS metadata API to identify any sensitive information disclosed
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The application makes a request to the AWS IMDS metadata API and discloses sensitive information, such as instance IDs or metadata about the underlying infrastructure.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: SSRF: Port Scanning Internal Network via Search by Price
phase2_test_cases.description: This test case attempts to exploit the SSRF vulnerability in the Mobile application's search functionality to perform port scanning on internal networks.
preconditions: The mobile application is installed and running on a device
test_steps: Step 1: Launch the mobile application and navigate to the Search by Price feature
Step 2: Craft a malicious search query that includes an SSRF payload, for example, `http://10.0.0.1:22/`
Step 3: Submit the search query and observe the application's response
Step 4: Verify that the application makes a request to the internal IP address (10.0.0.1:22) instead of the expected external API endpoint
Step 5: Analyze the response from the internal IP address to identify any open ports or services
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The application makes a request to the internal IP address (10.0.0.1:22) and discloses sensitive information, such as open ports or services on the internal network.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Default Credentials Enabled for Mobile App Search by Destination
phase2_test_cases.description: This test case attempts to exploit default credentials enabled for mobile app search by destination functionality.
test_steps: Step 1: Launch the mobile app and navigate to the search by destination feature
Step 2: Inspect the network traffic using a proxy tool (e.g., Burp Suite) or built-in developer tools (e.g., Chrome DevTools)
Step 3: Identify any default credentials or unneeded features enabled for the search functionality
Step 4: Attempt to access the search feature with custom credentials or modified parameters to simulate an attack scenario
Step 5: Verify that the app allows unauthorized access or reveals sensitive information due to default credentials being enabled
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The mobile app allows unauthorized access or reveals sensitive information due to default credentials being enabled, indicating a security misconfiguration.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Missing Security Headers for Mobile App Search by Destination
phase2_test_cases.description: This test case attempts to exploit missing security headers (HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options) for mobile app search by destination functionality.
test_steps: Step 1: Launch the mobile app and navigate to the search by destination feature
Step 2: Inspect the HTTP response headers using a proxy tool (e.g., Burp Suite) or built-in developer tools (e.g., Chrome DevTools)
Step 3: Verify that the response headers do not include HSTS, CSP, or X-Frame-Options security headers
Step 4: Attempt to inject malicious content (e.g., JavaScript) into the search feature using a proxy tool or built-in developer tools
Step 5: Verify that the app does not properly block or sanitize injected content due to missing security headers
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The mobile app fails to block or sanitize injected malicious content, indicating a security misconfiguration due to missing security headers.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Verbose Error Messages Revealing Stack Traces for Mobile App Search by Destination
phase2_test_cases.description: This test case attempts to exploit verbose error messages revealing stack traces or system information for mobile app search by destination functionality.
test_steps: Step 1: Launch the mobile app and navigate to the search by destination feature
Step 2: Intentionally trigger an error condition (e.g., invalid input) in the search feature
Step 3: Inspect the HTTP response headers using a proxy tool (e.g., Burp Suite) or built-in developer tools (e.g., Chrome DevTools)
Step 4: Verify that the response includes verbose error messages revealing stack traces or system information
Step 5: Attempt to extract sensitive information from the error message, such as database credentials or system paths
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The mobile app returns verbose error messages revealing stack traces or system information, indicating a security misconfiguration.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Reflected XSS in Search Parameters
phase2_test_cases.description: Exploit reflected XSS vulnerability by injecting malicious JavaScript code into search parameters.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the search page at /search
Step 2: Intercept the search request and modify the 'q' parameter to contain payload: <script>alert(document.cookie)</script>
Step 3: Submit the modified request and verify HTTP 200 response
Step 4: Verify that the JavaScript payload executes and displays an alert box when viewing the search results page at /search/results
Step 5: Repeat steps 2-4 with different payloads to ensure exploit is consistent
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The JavaScript payload executes and displays an alert box when viewing the search results page, indicating successful exploitation of reflected XSS vulnerability.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Stored XSS in Comments
phase2_test_cases.description: Exploit stored XSS vulnerability by injecting malicious JavaScript code into comments.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the comment section at /comments
Step 2: Interact with a comment form and inject payload: <script>alert(document.cookie)</script> into the comment field
Step 3: Submit the comment and verify HTTP 200 response
Step 4: Verify that the JavaScript payload executes and displays an alert box when viewing the commented page at /comments/<comment_id>
Step 5: Repeat steps 2-4 with different payloads to ensure exploit is consistent
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The JavaScript payload executes and displays an alert box when viewing the commented page, indicating successful exploitation of stored XSS vulnerability.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: DOM-based XSS via File Upload
phase2_test_cases.description: Exploit DOM-based XSS vulnerability by injecting malicious JavaScript code into file uploads.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the file upload page at /upload
Step 2: Interact with a file upload form and inject payload: <script>alert(document.cookie)</script> into the file description field
Step 3: Upload a malicious file and verify HTTP 200 response
Step 4: Verify that the JavaScript payload executes and displays an alert box when viewing the uploaded file at /upload/<file_name>
Step 5: Repeat steps 2-4 with different payloads to ensure exploit is consistent
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The JavaScript payload executes and displays an alert box when viewing the uploaded file, indicating successful exploitation of DOM-based XSS vulnerability.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Outdated OpenSSL Library Exploit
phase2_test_cases.description: This test attempts to exploit a known CVE in the OpenSSL library used by the mobile application.
preconditions: The application uses an outdated version of OpenSSL
test_steps: Step 1: Identify the OpenSSL version used by the application through reconnaissance or manual review of code
Step 2: Craft a custom SSL/TLS handshake packet using the identified CVE's attack vector (e.g., TLS 1.0 CBC padding oracle)
Step 3: Send the crafted packet to the application's SSL/TLS endpoint and verify that it is accepted
Step 4: Analyze the application's response to determine if the exploit was successful (e.g., decryption of sensitive data)
Step 5: Verify that the exploit can be repeated multiple times without being detected or mitigated
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: Successful decryption of sensitive data (e.g., user credentials) within 5 attempts, indicating a high likelihood of exploitation.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: SSRF: Internal IP Scanning via Search by Destination
phase2_test_cases.description: Exploit the SSRF vulnerability to scan internal IPs and identify potential hosts on the network.
preconditions: The application is vulnerable to SSRF
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the search page at /search/destination
Step 2: Craft a payload by appending an internal IP address (e.g., 10.0.0.1) to the destination parameter in the URL
Step 3: Send the modified request and verify that the application makes a request to the internal IP address
Step 4: Observe the response from the internal host and identify potential hosts on the network
Step 5: Verify that the identified hosts are not publicly accessible or exposed
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: Identification of at least 3 internal IP addresses that are not publicly accessible or exposed, with specific criteria including no open ports or services.

Step 2: Craft a payload by appending the AWS IMDS endpoint (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/) to the destination parameter in the URL
Step 3: Send the modified request and verify that the application makes a request to the AWS IMDS endpoint
Step 4: Observe the response from the AWS IMDS API and retrieve information about the underlying infrastructure (e.g., instance ID, availability zone)

phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The log entry does not contain any information about the malicious User-Agent string, indicating a lack of auditing for server errors.

phase2_test_cases.description: This test case attempts to exploit the SSRF vulnerability in the mobile application's search functionality to disclose internal IP addresses.

Step 3: Verify that the API does not return the HSTS header or returns it with a short max-age value (e.g., < 31536000 seconds)

Step 3: Modify the query parameter to contain a different internal resource URL (e.g., http://localhost:8081/anther-secret-resource) and re-send the GET request.
Step 4: Verify that the server responds with a HTTP 200 status code and the requested internal resource is returned in the response again.
Step 5: Repeat Step 3-4 for multiple internal resources to confirm SSRF vulnerability.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Internal IP Address Disclosure via SSRF
phase2_test_cases.description: This test case attempts to force the server to make requests to internal resources, potentially disclosing internal IP addresses.
preconditions: The mobile application is vulnerable to SSRF
test_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to the vulnerable endpoint with the parameter 'internal_ip' set to an internal IP address (e.g., 10.0.0.1).
Step 2: Verify that the server responds with a HTTP 200 status code and the response contains the requested internal IP address.
Step 3: Repeat Step 1 with different internal IP addresses to confirm the vulnerability.
Step 4: Use the disclosed internal IP addresses to identify potential attack vectors, such as port scanning or accessing metadata APIs (e.g., AWS IMDS).
Step 5: Verify that the server does not properly sanitize user input, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary requests.
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The server responds with a HTTP 200 status code and the response contains the requested internal IP address (e.g., 10.0.0.1). The vulnerability is confirmed by successfully disclosing multiple internal IP addresses.

Step 2: Intercept the search request and modify the 'q' parameter to contain payload: <script>alert(document.cookie)</script>
Step 3: Submit the modified request and verify HTTP 200 response
Step 4: Verify that the JavaScript payload executes and displays an alert box when viewing search results
Step 5: Repeat steps 2-4 with different payloads to confirm exploit
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The JavaScript payload executes and displays an alert box when viewing search results, indicating successful exploitation of reflected XSS vulnerability.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Stored XSS in Comments
phase2_test_cases.description: Exploit stored XSS vulnerability by injecting malicious JavaScript code into comments.
test_steps: Step 1: Navigate to a public profile page at /profile/view
Step 2: Post a comment with payload: <script>alert(document.cookie)</script>
Step 3: Verify that the comment is successfully posted and displayed on the profile page
Step 4: Inspect the HTML source of the comment section to confirm presence of malicious JavaScript code
Step 5: Verify that the JavaScript payload executes and displays an alert box when viewing the comment
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The JavaScript payload executes and displays an alert box when viewing the comment, indicating successful exploitation of stored XSS vulnerability.

Step 2: Upload a file with payload: <script>alert(document.cookie)</script> as metadata (e.g., file description)

Step 5: Verify that the JavaScript payload executes and displays an alert box when viewing the uploaded file

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Java Deserialization RCE via Profile Update

Step 2: Intercept the profile update POST request and modify the 'bio' parameter to contain a malicious Java deserialization payload
Step 3: Submit the request and verify HTTP 200 response
Step 4: Observe application behavior and verify that the payload is executed, allowing for RCE
Step 5: Verify that the RCE allows for privilege escalation or other malicious actions
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: Successful execution of malicious Java code, allowing for RCE and potential privilege escalation (minimum 30 characters)

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Outdated OpenSSL Library Exploit
phase2_test_cases.description: This test attempts to exploit the outdated OpenSSL library vulnerability (CVE-2014-3513) in the mobile application's profile page.
preconditions: The mobile application is using an outdated version of OpenSSL
test_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to the /profile/view endpoint to retrieve the user's public profile information
Step 2: Inspect the response headers for the presence of the 'Strict-Transport-Security' header
Step 3: Modify the 'User-Agent' header to include the string 'Mozilla/4.0 (compatible; MSIE 8.0; Windows NT 5.1; Trident/4.0)' to trigger the outdated OpenSSL library
Step 4: Send a POST request to the /profile/edit endpoint with a modified 'bio' parameter containing the payload '<script>alert(document.cookie)</script>'
Step 5: Verify that the application responds with an HTTP 200 status code and executes the JavaScript payload
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The application executes the JavaScript payload, displaying an alert box containing the user's cookie information (minimum 30 characters)

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Unpatched Apache Struts Vulnerability Exploit

preconditions: The mobile application is using an unpatched version of Apache Struts

Step 2: Inspect the response headers for the presence of the 'X-Powered-By' header indicating the use of Apache Struts
Step 3: Craft a payload using the OWASP WebGoat Struts2 Example exploit (https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Struts_2_XSS_Vulnerability)
Step 4: Send a POST request to the /profile/edit endpoint with the crafted payload
Step 5: Verify that the application responds with an HTTP 200 status code and executes the JavaScript payload
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The application executes the JavaScript payload, displaying an alert box containing the user's cookie information (minimum 30 characters)

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Login Log Injection Vulnerability

test_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the login endpoint at /login with username 'test' and password 'test'
Step 2: Modify the 'username' parameter in the request body to contain the payload: OR 1=1 --
Step 3: Submit the modified request and verify that the application logs the successful login attempt
Step 4: Verify that the log entry contains the injected payload
Step 5: Check the application's logs for any suspicious or unusual activity
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The log entry should contain the injected payload, and the application should not detect any suspicious activity.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Brute Force Attack Alert Failure

Step 2: Verify that the application logs each failed login attempt
Step 3: Wait for 30 seconds and then send another series of login requests with incorrect credentials
Step 4: Verify that the application still logs each failed login attempt without alerting on the brute force attack
Step 5: Repeat steps 1-4 to verify that the vulnerability is exploitable
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The application should not detect any suspicious activity or alert on the brute force attack.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Internal Resource Access via Profile Page

Step 2: Identify the JavaScript file responsible for loading the profile data (e.g., /js/profile.js)
Step 3: Modify the 'src' attribute of the script tag to point to an internal resource, such as /internal/api/userinfo
Step 4: Submit the modified request and verify that the server responds with the requested internal resource's metadata
Step 5: Verify that the received data contains sensitive information about the internal resource
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The server returns the requested internal resource's metadata, indicating successful SSRF exploitation.

Step 2: Identify the JavaScript file responsible for loading the profile data (e.g., /js/profile.js)
Step 3: Modify the 'src' attribute of the script tag to point to the AWS IMDS endpoint, such as https://metadata.aws.amazon.com/imds/2/latest/meta-info.json
Step 4: Submit the modified request and verify that the server responds with the requested IMDS metadata
Step 5: Verify that the received data contains sensitive information about the AWS instance
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The server returns the requested IMDS metadata, indicating successful SSRF exploitation and potential access to sensitive AWS instance information.

phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The server returns the requested internal network service's metadata, indicating successful SSRF exploitation and potential access to sensitive internal network information.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Default Credentials Enabled for Restaurant Account Creation
phase2_test_cases.description: This test case attempts to exploit default credentials enabled for restaurant account creation.

Step 2: Inspect the HTML source code and look for any hints of default credentials being used (e.g., hardcoded admin username/password)

Step 5: Verify that the created account has administrative privileges
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The test case expects to successfully create an admin-level account using default credentials, with a specific username and password.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: Missing Security Headers for Restaurant Account Creation

Step 2: Inspect the HTTP response headers and look for any missing security headers (e.g., HSTS, CSP)
Step 3: Craft a payload using the discovered missing security header (e.g., set 'Content-Security-Policy' to 'default-src https://evil.com')
Step 4: Submit the payload to the create account form and verify HTTP 200 response
Step 5: Verify that the created account has administrative privileges
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The test case expects to successfully create an admin-level account with missing security headers, resulting in potential XSS or other attacks.

Step 3: Inspect the HTTP response headers and look for any verbose error messages revealing system information (e.g., server version, OS)

phase2_test_cases.test_title: SSRF: Forcing Server to Make Requests to Internal Resources
phase2_test_cases.description: Exploit the vulnerability by forcing the server to make requests to internal resources, such as metadata APIs or port scanning internal networks.
preconditions: The application is vulnerable to SSRF
test_steps: Step 1: Identify a vulnerable endpoint in the application, such as a URL parameter or HTTP header
Step 2: Craft a payload that forces the server to make requests to an internal resource, such as AWS IMDS (https://metadata.services.imds.amazonaws.com/latest/dynamic/instance-identity-document.json)
Step 3: Deliver the payload by sending a request to the vulnerable endpoint with the crafted payload
Step 4: Observe the application's behavior and verify that it makes requests to the internal resource
Step 5: Verify that the server returns metadata or other sensitive information
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The server returns metadata or other sensitive information, indicating successful exploitation of the SSRF vulnerability.

phase2_test_cases.test_title: SSRF: Port Scanning Internal Networks via the Vulnerable Application
phase2_test_cases.description: Exploit the vulnerability by forcing the server to make requests to internal networks, allowing for port scanning and potential discovery of sensitive services.
preconditions: The application is vulnerable to SSRF
test_steps: Step 1: Identify a vulnerable endpoint in the application, such as a URL parameter or HTTP header
Step 2: Craft a payload that forces the server to make requests to an internal network IP address and port range (e.g., 192.168.1.0/24:1-65535)
Step 3: Deliver the payload by sending a request to the vulnerable endpoint with the crafted payload
Step 4: Observe the application's behavior and verify that it makes requests to the internal network IP address and port range
Step 5: Verify that the server returns information about open ports or services
phase2_test_cases.expected_result: The server returns information about open ports or services, indicating successful exploitation of the SSRF vulnerability.
